turns.content: Hey, I'm preparing for a Rust interview and want to understand blanket implementations. Can you explain how they work? Specifically, when I see something like `impl<T: Display> Print for T;` in the standard library—what's going on here?

Sure! So imagine you have a trait `Print`, and then Rust lets you say 'for any type that implements `Display`, this type automatically gets to implement `Print`.' It’s like saying, 'if you can print something as text (that's what `Display` does), then you can also print it in our special way too!'
OK, so that makes sense. But I’m curious about the standard library blanket impl: `impl<T: Clone> From<&T> for T;`. What’s the deal with this?

Well, think of it like automatic conversion. If you have a reference to something that can clone itself (`Clone`), Rust lets you easily get the owned version by just dereferencing and cloning. It’s like saying 'if you know how to copy this thing, then you can also get a new copy of it!'
I see. But what happens if I try to implement my own blanket impl? Like `impl<T: Display> Print for T;` again on the same trait? Will that work?

Hmm, good question! Rust has a rule called the 'orphan rule.' It means you can only implement a trait on your own types or traits from other crates that you import. So if `Print` is part of a crate you depend on, you can’t blanket impl it for all `T`. That’s why the standard library uses their own internal traits.
That makes sense—so I can’t override blanket implementations from other crates?
Exactly! And that’s where you have to be careful. If you want your type to behave a certain way, sometimes you might need to define your own trait and implement it explicitly.
So what about when I’m extending existing types? Like, say `impl<T: Clone> From<&T> for T;`—could this conflict with future upstream changes?

Good point. Since the standard library blanket impl is in a crate you depend on, your code could break if they change their implementation. That’s why it’s safer to avoid blanket implementations unless you’re 100% sure.
Right, so I should stick with explicit trait implementations then?
Yes! That way, your code won’t break unexpectedly. And it’s clearer what exactly is going on.
Okay, so when I want to extend a type with blanket impls from other crates—say `impl<T: Display> Print for T;`—is there any way around the orphan rule?
Nope. The orphan rule is there for a reason—it’s meant to prevent ambiguity and make your code clearer. If you really want to extend something, define your own trait or use existing ones.
Alright, thanks! That clears things up—blanket implementations are powerful but tricky if I’m extending other crates.
You're welcome! Always feel free to ask if you have more questions. Good luck with your interview!
